This course is designed to introduce students to the ideas and nature of entrepreneurship, and to instill the knowledge and skills needed to navigate starting and managing a new business.

Course overview:

This course is designed to give students a solid foundation of understanding the process of entrepreneurship. The course is divided into three broad topics: (1) the theory(ies) of entrepreneurship, (2) the process of ideation and innovation, and (3) strategies for managing a successful venture. 

Because entrepreneurship is a skillset, it is not easily learned by instruction. The goal of this course, then, is not to lecture but to engage students in an active learning process. Its assignments are designed to help students learn by doing. By the end of the class, students will have engineered and developed an idea all the way up to the point of launching a venture. While students will not be required to legally start a business, they should be ready to do so, if they so desire, as soon as the course is over.

Numerical modeling software for geotechnical analyses of soil, rock, groundwater, and ground support. The students will learn design of geotechnical excavations (e.g., slopes, tunnels, caverns, stopes, etc.) and constructs (dams, foundations, footings, walls, etc.) in soil, and intact rock (i.e., heavily jointed rock). Such analyses include design, factor of safety prediction, and back-analysis of failure.

Starting, managing a franchise business: characteristics of franchisor and franchisee; evaluation of franchising opportunities; legal concerns of franchising; development strategies and successful planning, implementation and launching of a new business. We¿ll look at how U.S. policies during the Cold War, as well as contemporary politics, have fueled cycles of violence and contributed to a transnational crisis between the U.S. and the Northern Triangle of Central America.
Students will review classical works and recent literature on rangeland ecological theory and examine scenarios through the lenses of various rangeland ecological theories.
Theories, methods, and practice to perform multilevel modeling statistics in educational research. Using statistics programs for the data analysis

REM490 teaches students how to plan a project based on analysis of observations and data collected from a field site. Planning involves communication skills to discuss and negotiate with stakeholders, set objectives, determine actions with supporting rationale, scheduling & budgeting, monitoring, and contingency planning.

Learn about great sports storytelling from masters of the journalistic art as well as from filmmakers and other creative practitioners. Consider how journalists can enrich their work by exploring narrative styles rather than limiting themselves to expository approaches. Study how screen and fiction writers understand narrative elements and explore the challenges of incorporating these elements into reporting and writing true stories that adhere to the highest standards of journalistic practice.
Empirical research on a specific project under supervision of university faculty.

This course concentrates on the research process in mass communication. It is designed to provide you with a broad understanding of research concepts, processes, techniques, and strategies of media research. We will examine research conducted in both the professional and academic setting, helping you to become intelligent consumers of social science research. The course will include instruction in quantitative and qualitative methods, covering both research design and data collection. The overall purpose of this course is to help you understand the role of research in media institutions and the mass communication process. Upon completing this course, you should be able to evaluate and interpret mass communication research and understand the uses and benefits of various methodological approaches.
Understanding the mineral processes of sampling, liberation, size reduction with unit process selection.

This course will cover the basics of Python programming and the common Python libraries used in scientific research. While examples will draw primarily from the Earth Sciences, the skills learned are readily applicable to other disciplines; students from other programs are most welcome to take this course. The course is divided into four sections: Python Fundamentals, Scientific Python, Data Visualizations, and Introduction to Machine Learning.

This course will cover an introduction to transportation planning in urban areas. The multi-disciplinary nature of planning as an area for professional practice in the geographical, socio-economic and political contexts of the U.S. will be stressed. Furthermore, this course will examine history of urban transportation planning, planning decisions and their ability to influence stakeholder priorities, and transportation problems as related to urban areas; transit and other mode.

An introduction to the field of environmental, science, health and risk communication. Class discussions will draw theoretical and conceptual considerations from fields such as environmental sociology, social psychology, visual communication, and risk perception. Topics include, for example, social constructions of environmental issues, environmental discourses in popular culture, scientific uncertainty, pseudoscience and misinformation, technology and system failures, health emergency.

An approach of meta-design for instructional design and assessment. Exploration of methods and innovative applications to formulate new frameworks of dynamic design and assessment in the field of instructional technology and learning design.

column18: Data Driven Design and Modeling for Instructions

Ethnography is the systematic observation of human life to produce sociological knowledge and theory. This course guides students through readings of classic and recent texts, focusing on the relationships between research and writing, description and interpretation, and narrative and theory. Students will also gain experience in the practice of ethnography through workshops on ethics, positionality, power differentials, collecting data, writing field notes, analysis, and crafting claims.

column18: Life, Death, and Sex: Social Demography
column28: Examines the influences of demography (births, deaths, and mobility) on socioeconomic and political structures. Several topics covered affecting national and international populations such as race/ethnicity, births, deaths, gender, family, socioeconomic opportunities, poverty, aging, immigration, migration, urbanization, and environment. Links between these trends, policy, and well-being are established.

This course covers the fundamentals of creative writing. Students practice a variety of writing forms and receive feedback on their work from their mentor. Additional topics for feedback may include sensory and figurative description, sound, line, tone and voice.

Environmental scientist and ecologists are increasingly asked to predict ecological processes to improve the sustainable management of natural resources and further basic understanding. This course will cover the fundamental concepts of the emerging field of ecological forecasting including: modeling approaches, representing and propagating uncertainty, model validation and updating, and adaptive management.

This course provides an opportunity for students to expand their ASL skills acquired during levels 1-4 and to pursue greater fluency with the language. Students will incorporate more nuanced sentence structures into ASL narratives and implement the use of multiple character roles. They will further broaden their vocabulary base, and gain the language experience necessary to sign about sensitive topics like important moments, facts, rules, and personally significant events.

An analysis of ASL structure, its morphology, syntax, and change over time. This course examines the unique modality of signed languages, and includes a comparison to other linguistics structures, as well as accents and regional variations.

This course provides a broad understanding of the theoretical foundation of media content, including message delivery, development, and consumption. We will examine the role of theory in media institutions and the mass communication process, helping you to become intelligent consumers of media. Upon completing this course, you should be able to evaluate and interpret the influence and impact of mass communication and media as it relates to a variety of different contexts.

Focuses on the freight journey of global supply chain management. You will learn intermodal flows (truck, rail, ship, and air), customs clearance, post-clearance delivery, and how transportation links partners and facilities separated geographically in a global supply chain. Topics include: Global Business and Transportation Economics; Transportation Modes and Sourcing Requirements; Transportation Technology, Systems and Tools; Risk Management, Sustainability, and Innovation.

column28: Physiology of the, integumentary, skeletal, muscular, and nervous systems.  Primarily for pre-professional students pursuing careers in medicine, dentistry, and other professional degree programs.  This course may be used for credit towards the major in Biology.

column18: Human Anatomy and Physiology for Pre-Professionals II
column28: Circulatory, immune, respiratory, endocrine, digestive, urinary and reproductive systems physiology. Primarily for pre-professional students pursuing careers in medicine, dentistry, and other professional degree programs. This course may be used for credit towards the major in Biology.

This classroom and field-based course trains students to be in a leadership role with instruction in outdoor leadership competencies, planning, preparation, route selection, equipment use, navigation techniques, leave no-trace ethics, risk management, and group facilitation skills. Students must be able to walk for extended periods while carrying a 50-60lb backpack through steep and rugged terrain.

This seminar considers international relations through the case of Korea from 1864 to 1945. A united monarchy since 936, Korea resisted imperialism and colonialism as great powers imposed unequal treaties. An effort to build a modern nation-state saw progress, but Japan¿s defeat of China and Russia while securing support from the United States and Britain sealed Korea¿s fate. This course explores the complexities of modernity vis-à-vis global power dynamics and identity transformations.
Introduction to Indigenous education with a focus at a local level.

This course examines how the body operates as a rhetorical phenomenon, investigates the presentation of bodies in discourse and media, and critiques ways of categorizing bodies. The course engages and evaluates different critical and humanistic theories about the body and discusses such topics as disability, sexuality, gender, Illness, mortality, and normalization.
Examines theories and topics in rhetoric in the communication studies discipline. Repeatable if topics differ.

Explores theoretical and methodological approaches of community engaged scholarship in communication studies including communication activism research, participatory action research, service-learning, and civic engagement.

This doctoral seminar is aimed at reading, understanding, and conducting research in human resource management (HRM). Students should obtain a thorough understanding of the traditional topics of HRM (e.g., recruitment and selection, performance management, compensation) as well as the strategic perspective of HR (e.g., human capital, HR system, star performers). They should also develop the skills necessary to evaluate, criticize, and contribute to HRM literature.
